Concrete bug: ShutdownSignal::trigger_immediate() currently checks phase() != Stopped and then unconditionally stores ForceClosing.  {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331.18","title":"[audit][grpc][health] HealthWatcher must report transient service changes between polls","description":"Focused audit child under asupersync-1qfd0.331 for src/grpc/health.rs only. HealthWatcher currently tracks only the last observed ServingStatus. If a watched service flips away from a status and back again before the next poll, changed()/poll_status() report no change even though the watched service did change in the interim.  {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331.2","title":"[audit][grpc] Keep shared service health registered until last HealthReporter drops","description":"Audit follow-up in src/grpc/health.rs. HealthReporter currently clears its service status unconditionally on Drop. If multiple HealthReporter instances exist for the same service, dropping any one of them removes the shared registration even while another reporter for that same service is still alive and may still be marking it serving/not-serving. Scope: retain per-service reporter ownership so only the final reporter drop clears status, add focused regression coverage for multi-reporter lifecycle semantics, and validate with rch-backed targeted tests/checks.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"EmeraldForge","created_at":"2026-03-13T07:23:31.116168820Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-13T07:39:02.938626779Z","closed_at":"2026-03-13T07:39:02.938585803Z","close_reason":"Fixed grpc/health multi-reporter lifecycle bug; added regression; targeted remote test and remote cargo check passed; fmt check passed; remote clippy blocked by rch instability","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331.2","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-13T07:23:31.116168820Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331.20","title":"[audit][dns] Honor negative_ttl by caching no-record DNS failures","description":"Focused audit child under asupersync-1qfd0.331 for src/net/dns/cache.rs and src/net/dns/resolver.rs only. CacheConfig exposes negative_ttl for NXDOMAIN/no-record answers, but the Phase-0 resolver/cache path never stores or serves negative entries, so repeated no-record lookups always hit blocking resolution and the config field is effectively ignored. Scope: classify no-record resolution failures fail-closed, cache them using negative_ttl, add focused regressions for repeated negative lookups and expiration, and validate with rch-backed cargo gates plus local hygiene.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"RoseIsland","created_at":"2026-03-15T06:04:57.008669524Z","created_by":"CopperSpring","updated_at":"2026-03-17T02:34:20.377847520Z","closed_at":"2026-03-17T02:34:20.377818656Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331.20","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.331","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-15T06:04:57.008669524Z","created_by":"CopperSpring","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.395","title":"[audit][link] Self-link establishment must not duplicate self-exit actions","description":"Focused audit follow-up for src/link.rs only. LinkSet::establish_with_policy() currently allows task_a == task_b and indexes the same LinkRef twice in task_index. That makes a single logical self-link appear twice in peers_of()/peers_with_policy() and causes resolve_exits() to emit duplicate self-exit actions for one abnormal termination. Scope: make self-links fail closed or otherwise canonicalize them so one logical self-link cannot duplicate peer/exit behavior, add focused regressions, and keep the write surface to src/link.rs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"RoseIsland","created_at":"2026-03-17T01:08:56.848727377Z","created_by":"RoseIsland","updated_at":"2026-03-17T01:14:19.447183099Z","closed_at":"2026-03-17T01:14:19.447153464Z","close_reason":"Patched src/link.rs so self-links are indexed once in task_index, eliminating duplicate peers_with_policy/resolve_exits behavior. Added regressions self_link_is_indexed_once_in_peer_views and resolve_exits_self_link_emits_single_action. Validation: rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch-roseisland-link-test cargo test -p asupersync self_link --lib passed. cargo fmt --check is blocked by unrelated formatting drift in asupersync-tokio-compat/src/body_bridge.rs; cargo check --all-targets is blocked by unrelated tests/e2e_raptorq_pipeline.rs API drift; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings is blocked by unrelated src/process.rs if_not_else lint.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","link","spork"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.395","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T01:08:56.848727377Z","created_by":"RoseIsland","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.396","title":"[audit][link] Self-link establishment must not duplicate self-exit actions","description":"Focused audit follow-up for src/link.rs only. LinkSet::establish_with_policy() currently allows task_a == task_b and indexes the same LinkRef twice in task_index. That makes a single logical self-link appear twice in peers_of()/peers_with_policy() and causes resolve_exits() to emit duplicate self-exit actions for one abnormal termination. Scope: make self-links fail closed or otherwise canonicalize them so one logical self-link cannot duplicate peer/exit behavior, add focused regressions, and keep the write surface to src/link.rs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"ChartreuseBeaver","created_at":"2026-03-17T01:08:56.848727377Z","created_by":"RoseIsland","updated_at":"2026-03-18T06:34:22.264563078Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T06:34:22.264535207Z","close_reason":"Already fixed in current tree; stale-open after prior audit/fix landed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","link","spork"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.396","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T01:08:56.848727377Z","created_by":"RoseIsland","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.397","title":"[audit][reactor] Registration::deregister must preserve Drop cleanup after persistent deregister failure","description":"Fresh diff audit of src/runtime/reactor/registration.rs found that Registration::deregister(self) consumed the handle but still disarmed Drop even when both explicit deregister attempts failed or the reactor panicked. That leaves callers with no retry surface and can strand reactor bookkeeping instead of preserving one final best-effort cleanup pass. Patch should keep Drop armed on persistent explicit deregister failure, add focused regression coverage, and validate with rch-offloaded cargo commands.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaStone","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:16:02.736764796Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-17T02:31:08.359563488Z","closed_at":"2026-03-17T02:31:08.359534123Z","close_reason":"Completed: kept Registration Drop armed on persistent explicit deregister failure/panic, added focused regressions, validated via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","reactor"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.397","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:16:02.736764796Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2524,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.397","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Patched src/runtime/reactor/registration.rs so Registration::deregister(self) only disarms Drop on successful explicit cleanup. Previously the consumed handle still disarmed Drop after persistent deregister error or panic, which removed the last best-effort cleanup path and could strand reactor bookkeeping.

Validation:
- git diff --check passed
- rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch-fuchsiastone-registration cargo check -p asupersync --lib passed
- rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch-fuchsiastone-registration cargo test -p asupersync --lib explicit_deregister_ -- --nocapture passed with 5 tests
- rch exec -- rustfmt --check src/runtime/reactor/registration.rs passed after fixing import ordering
- focused cargo clippy on the lib is currently blocked by unrelated concurrent DNS work in src/net/dns/resolver.rs:259 (clippy needless pass by value), not by this patch
- repo-wide cargo fmt --check is currently red because concurrent edits in src/net/dns/cache.rs and src/net/dns/resolver.rs are not yet rustfmt-clean

Regression coverage:
- strengthened persistent-error test to prove Drop stays armed and performs the final cleanup pass
- strengthened panic-path test to prove the post-error Drop retry occurs
- added explicit_deregister_error_still_allows_drop_cleanup_success, where the first two explicit deregister attempts fail and the final Drop cleanup attempt succeeds","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:30:33Z"}]}
-{"id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","title":"[audit][dns] Canonicalize trailing-dot FQDNs in resolver/cache","description":"Root cause:
- resolver host validation rejects standards-valid absolute hostnames when the only extra byte is the trailing root dot
- cache key normalization lowercases hosts but does not canonicalize a trailing dot, so example.com and example.com. fragment cache/negative-cache behavior

Planned fix:
- canonicalize DNS cache/resolver host keys so trailing-dot FQDNs share cache entries with the non-dotted form
- accept max-length absolute hostnames by validating the canonical hostname length instead of the raw dotted input
- add focused regressions for cache aliasing and absolute-hostname length handling","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"TurquoiseHawk","created_at":"2026-03-18T14:59:04.121334149Z","created_by":"RainyRiver","updated_at":"2026-03-18T23:23:39.123970759Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T23:23:39.123946414Z","close_reason":"Completed: current HEAD contains the DNS absolute-name cache fix plus regressions; actual bug was trailing-dot cache aliasing in resolver lookup/store, while strict clippy/fmt failures remain outside this slice.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","dns"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","depends_on_id":"asupersync-1qfd0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-18T14:59:04.121334149Z","created_by":"RainyRiver","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2544,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"RainyRiver","text":"Start note:
- `bv --robot-triage` and `bv --robot-next` both timed out again in this repo, so I used the ready set plus live code inspection to keep momentum.
- Concrete bug is in DNS canonicalization, not another stale already-landed bead.

Root cause under fix:
- trailing-dot FQDNs fragment resolver/cache behavior because cache key normalization only lowercases
- resolver length validation checks the raw host string before canonicalizing away the trailing root dot","created_at":"2026-03-18T14:59:28Z"},{"id":2545,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"RainyRiver","text":"Scope correction after deeper DNS semantics review:
- I am **not** canonicalizing trailing-dot and non-dotted names into one cache key; that would risk changing search-domain semantics.
- The concrete live bug is narrower and real: `Resolver::do_lookup_ip()` rejected a standards-valid max-length absolute hostname because it counted the trailing root dot before validation.
- Fix in flight keeps query semantics intact and only validates length against the hostname with one trailing root dot trimmed.","created_at":"2026-03-18T15:01:58Z"},{"id":2546,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"RainyRiver","text":"Progress update:
- landed the resolver-side fix in `src/net/dns/resolver.rs`: max-length absolute hostnames now validate after trimming one trailing root dot for length-check purposes only
- added focused regressions for the standards-valid 254-byte absolute hostname and the still-invalid overlong case

Validation status:
- `rustfmt --edition 2024 --check src/net/dns/resolver.rs` passed
- `ubs src/net/dns/resolver.rs` exited 0
- `rch exec -- ... cargo test --lib absolute_hostname -- --nocapture` and `rch exec -- ... cargo check --all-targets` are currently blocked by infrastructure, not code: the scheduler repeatedly routes to worker `vmi1227854`, and `rch fleet status` reports that worker at `0MB` free on `/tmp`, producing `cc` temp-file `ENOSPC` failures before compilation finishes

Reservation narrowed:
- released `src/net/dns/cache.rs`; keeping `src/net/dns/resolver.rs` reserved while the offloaded cargo gates are blocked","created_at":"2026-03-18T15:06:58Z"},{"id":2552,"issue_id":"asupersync-1qfd0.398","author":"TurquoiseHawk","text":"Fresh-eyes DNS audit update:
- confirmed the live bug is the opposite of the bead title: `Resolver::lookup_ip()` was stripping one trailing root dot before cache lookup/store, which aliased `name.` onto `name` and could leak search-domain-sensitive cache results across the absolute/non-absolute boundary
- kept trailing-dot trimming only for hostname-length validation in `do_lookup_ip()`; cache identity now stays on the original query host
- added focused regressions in `src/net/dns/resolver.rs` proving dotted queries do not reuse non-dotted positive or negative cache entries, plus a `src/net/dns/cache.rs` regression proving case-insensitive lookups still preserve dotted-vs-non-dotted key separation

Validation so far:
- `git diff --check -- src/net/dns/resolver.rs src/net/dns/cache.rs` PASS
- `ubs --only=rust src/net/dns` ran clean of new critical findings but is noisy on existing test unwrap/assert inventory and one unrelated lifecycle heuristic in resolver connect code
- `rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets` reached remote `exit=0`; local tail then reported the usual artifact-retrieval vanished-file warning
- `rch exec -- cargo fmt --check` is red outside this slice in `src/http/h1/http_client.rs`, `src/net/websocket/*`, and `tests/websocket_acceptor_trailing_bytes.rs`
- `rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` is red outside this slice in `src/stream/debounce.rs` and `src/net/websocket/handshake.rs`
- focused `rch` test lanes for the new DNS regressions are still running unusually slowly / quietly after compile on the remote worker; no DNS-specific failure has surfaced yet","created_at":"2026-03-18T23:20:33Z"}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-2c9j7.29","title":"[RaptorQ] Make Symbol::new_for_test source-by-default and add explicit repair helper","description":"# Summary
`Symbol::new_for_test(object, sbn, esi, data)` currently infers `SymbolKind` as `Source` only for `esi == 0` and `Repair` otherwise. That is not a valid RaptorQ rule because source-vs-repair depends on block K, not on whether ESI equals zero.

# Why this is a bug
Many tests use `new_for_test` as a generic symbol constructor for ordered source-like sequences (`esi = 0,1,2,...`). The current helper silently misclassifies every nonzero ESI as repair, which can distort `SymbolSet` accounting and any other test logic that inspects symbol kind.

# Plan
- make the default helper construct a source symbol
- add an explicit repair-specific test helper
- update only the call sites that truly need repair-kind semantics
- validate affected surfaces with `rch exec -- ...` cargo commands

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.2","title":"[FABRIC][P1] Core Subject Fabric","description":"# Phase 1: Core Subject Fabric

## Purpose
Build the subject primitive as a first-class runtime object with a Sublist-style routing engine, wildcard matching, interest state tracking, and subject semantic families. This is the foundation that all messaging, federation, and control-plane features build on.

## Key Components
1. **Subject Primitive** (src/messaging/subject.rs): Subject type with dot-separated token paths, SubjectSchema compilation, semantic family classification
2. **Sublist Routing Core**: Trie-based subject matching engine (inspired by NATS server/sublist.go) with generation-invalidated caches for literal-subject hot lookups and per-link hot caches
3. **Wildcard Matching**: Support for * (single token) and > (tail match) wildcards, compatible with NATS subject language
4. **Interest State Tracking**: Cancel-correct subscriber interest registration — cancelled subscribers must not leave ghost interest behind. Interest state changes are explicit events with trace-visible invalidation epochs.
5. **Queue Groups**: Multiple subscribers sharing work under one subject (one message per group delivery)

## Design Principles
- Subject fabric is for DISTRIBUTED naming and coordination, not a mandate that every local hot-path use subjects
- Direct channels, sessions, and GenServer calls remain correct for local hot-path interactions
- The subject fabric provides: distributed actor naming/rendezvous, service addressing, stream capture coordination, control-plane advisories, distributed federation
- All sharing one primary address model where distributed naming and policy matter

## Physical Design
- Actor-side mutation semantics own the canonical subject-index contract
- Index itself should be physically sharded and read-optimized, not funneled through one monolithic mutable authority
- Interest registration is cancel-correct via the existing cancel/drain/finalize protocol

## Relation to Existing Code
- transport/router.rs has RoutingTable with TTL, EndpointId routing — this extends that concept to subject-based addressing
- distributed/assignment.rs has placement concepts — subjects extend these with semantic families

## FABRIC North Star Reference
This phase is governed by the five cross-cutting FABRIC success criteria defined in `src/messaging/mod.rs` and `docs/FABRIC_GUARDRAILS.md`:
1. Keep the public mental model NATS-small.
2. Make stronger guarantees explicit named service classes, never hidden taxes on the common case.
3. Lower radical behavior into small, inspectable artifacts.
4. Keep autonomous policy loops inside declared safety envelopes with replay, evidence, and rollback.
5. Target the first product wedge at systems that need sovereignty, durable partial progress, and post-incident explanation together.

If work in this phase violates those criteria, or drifts toward the anti-goal of a grand unified fabric that makes the common case slower and less legible than NATS, it must be redesigned.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-17T06:54:34.834595046Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T06:18:14.378150623Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fabric","routing","subject"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T06:54:34.834595046Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:04:28.840685026Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.2.1","title":"[FABRIC][P1.1] Build Sublist trie-based routing engine","description":"## Overview
Build a trie-based subject matching engine in src/messaging/subject.rs (routing portion), inspired by NATS server/sublist.go.

## Requirements

### Subject Matching
- Subjects are dot-separated token paths (e.g., `foo.bar.baz`)
- Support literal match: exact token equality at each level
- Support single-token wildcard (`*`): matches exactly one token at that position
- Support tail wildcard (`>`): matches one or more tokens from that position to end of subject
- Examples: `foo.*` matches `foo.bar` but not `foo.bar.baz`; `foo.>` matches `foo.bar` and `foo.bar.baz`

### Trie Structure
- Internal trie nodes keyed by token string
- Each node has: children map, wildcard child (`*`), tail-wildcard child (`>`), and subscriber set
- Insert/remove operations maintain structural invariants (prune empty branches on remove)
- Lookup walks the trie, collecting all matching subscribers across literal, wildcard, and tail-wildcard branches

### Caching
- Generation-invalidated literal-subject hot caches: cache full match results for literal (no-wildcard) subjects
- Per-link hot caches: each link (connection/session) maintains its own cache of recently resolved subjects
- Cache entries tagged with a generation counter; any mutation to the trie bumps the generation, invalidating all caches
- Cache hit path should be sub-microsecond (simple HashMap lookup + generation check)

### Concurrency
- Thread-safe concurrent reads with write-serialized mutations
- Read path: lock-free for cached lookups (AtomicU64 generation check + cached result)
- Write path: single writer at a time (Mutex or similar), bumps generation after mutation
- No reader starvation: writes must not block reads for extended periods

### Cancel-Correctness
- Interest registration is cancel-correct: when a subscriber is cancelled (Cx cancelled, task dropped), its interest MUST be removed from the trie
- No ghost interest: a cancelled subscriber must never appear in match results
- Implementation: tie subscriber lifetime to a guard/handle that removes interest on drop
- Test: subscribe, cancel the subscriber's Cx, verify the subscriber no longer appears in any match results

### Queue Groups
- Multiple subscribers can register under the same subject with a queue group name
- For a given message, only ONE subscriber per queue group receives delivery
- Queue group selection should be round-robin or random (not always the same subscriber)
- A subscriber can be in at most one queue group per subject

### Performance
- O(tokens) lookup where tokens = number of dot-separated segments in the subject
- Cache hit should be sub-microsecond
- Benchmark: 1M lookups/sec for cached literal subjects on modern hardware

## File Location
src/messaging/subject.rs (routing portion)

## Tests (in this file or tests/)
- Literal match: exact match, no match, partial match
- Single wildcard: `*` at various positions, multiple wildcards
- Tail wildcard: `>` at various depths, `>` alone matches everything
- Combined: `foo.*.>`, `*.bar.>`, etc.
- Edge cases: empty subject, single token, very deep nesting (100+ levels)
- Queue groups: single group delivery, multiple groups, group removal
- Cache: hit/miss, invalidation on mutation, generation rollover
- Concurrency: concurrent subscribe/unsubscribe/lookup from multiple threads
- Cancel-correctness: subscribe, drop guard, verify removal
- At least 20+ test cases total","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:20:40.601661558Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-17T16:48:55.295690851Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fabric","routing","subject"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2.1","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:20:40.601661558Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2525,"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"NATS implementation reference: server/sublist.go implements the trie-based matching. Key patterns to adapt: (1) the Sublist struct with Insert/Match/Remove operations, (2) result caching with generation-based invalidation, (3) queue group handling where one subscriber per group receives each message, (4) the SublistResult type with subscription lists. The Asupersync version must additionally make interest registration cancel-correct — NATS doesn't have this because Go's GC handles cleanup. In Asupersync, a cancelled subscriber MUST NOT leave ghost interest behind, which means interest removal must participate in the cancel/drain/finalize protocol.","created_at":"2026-03-17T16:48:55Z"}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.2.2","title":"[FABRIC][P1.2] Subject index sharding and read optimization","description":"## Overview
Implement physical sharding of the subject index for read optimization in src/messaging/subject.rs. The index must NOT be funneled through one monolithic mutable authority — instead, it is physically sharded so that reads on different shards are fully independent.

## Requirements

### Shard Assignment
- Shard by subject prefix hash: take the first N tokens of a subject, hash them, and map to a shard index
- Number of shards configurable at construction time (default: num_cpus or a reasonable power of 2, e.g., 16)
- Shard assignment is deterministic: same subject always maps to same shard
- Hash function: use a fast, well-distributed hash (e.g., FxHash, AHash) — not cryptographic

### Read Path (Lock-Free for Cached Lookups)
- Each shard has its own generation counter (AtomicU64)
- Cached lookups check the shard's generation counter — if unchanged, return cached result directly
- No mutex acquisition on the read hot path for cache hits
- Read path for cache miss: acquire a read lock (RwLock) on the shard's trie, perform lookup, update cache
- Reads on different shards are fully independent — no cross-shard synchronization

### Write Path (Serialized Per-Shard)
- Write operations (subscribe, unsubscribe) acquire a write lock on the target shard only
- Writes to different shards can proceed concurrently
- After mutation, the shard's generation counter is bumped (Acquire/Release ordering)
- No global write lock — only the affected shard is locked

### Actor-Side Mutation Semantics
- The actor (GenServer, task, etc.) that owns a subscription is the canonical authority for mutation
- Subscribe/unsubscribe operations originate from the owning actor
- The subject index is the shared read-optimized projection of actor-owned state
- This means: actors do NOT read-then-write the index; they issue write commands and the index applies them

### Generation-Based Cache Coherence
- Each shard maintains its own monotonically increasing generation counter
- Cache entries store the generation at time of lookup
- On read, compare cached generation with current shard generation
- If generations match, cache hit; if not, re-lookup from trie and update cache
- Generation overflow: use wrapping arithmetic (u64 won't overflow in practice, but handle gracefully)

Implement SubjectSchema compilation and semantic family classification in src/messaging/subject.rs. Subject declarations compile into SubjectSchema artifacts with semantic family assignment. The family choice constrains admissible transforms, default service class, reply-space law, evidence policy, and mobility permissions.

## Requirements

### Semantic Families
Define the following subject semantic families (enum SubjectFamily):
- **Event**: fire-and-forget notifications (e.g., `system.events.user.created`)
- **Request**: request-reply pairs expecting a response (e.g., `api.users.get`)
- **Reply**: response subjects auto-generated for request-reply (e.g., `_INBOX.abc123`)
- **Stream**: ordered, potentially persistent message streams (e.g., `data.orders.us-east`)
- **Control**: internal control-plane subjects (e.g., `$SYS.server.ping`)
- **Service**: service discovery and health (e.g., `$SVC.auth.health`)
- **CaptureSelector**: subjects used as subscription patterns with wildcards for capture (e.g., `orders.*.status`)

### SubjectSchema
A SubjectSchema contains:
- `pattern`: the subject pattern string (may contain wildcards for CaptureSelector family)
- `family`: the semantic family (SubjectFamily enum)
- `service_class`: default QoS/service class (e.g., BestEffort, AtLeastOnce, ExactlyOnce)
- `reply_space_rule`: for Request family, how reply subjects are generated (e.g., InboxPrefix, Deterministic)
- `evidence_policy`: whether messages on this subject require evidence/audit trail (e.g., None, Log, Prove)
- `mobility`: whether the subject can be federated across cluster boundaries (Local, Federated, Global)
- `transforms`: list of admissible transforms (e.g., Map, Filter, Aggregate) — family constrains which are valid

### SubjectSchema Compilation
- `SubjectSchema::compile(declaration) -> Result<SubjectSchema, SchemaError>`
- Validates family-specific constraints:
  - Reply subjects MUST have a ReplySpaceRule
  - Control subjects MUST be in the `$SYS` namespace (pattern starts with `$SYS.`)
  - Service subjects MUST be in the `$SVC` namespace
  - CaptureSelector MUST contain at least one wildcard (`*` or `>`)
  - Event subjects MUST NOT have ExactlyOnce service class (fire-and-forget semantics)
  - Request subjects MUST have a reply_space_rule set
- Returns descriptive SchemaError on validation failure

### SubjectRegistry
- `SubjectRegistry::new() -> SubjectRegistry`
- `register(schema: SubjectSchema) -> Result<(), RegistryError>`: register a compiled schema; error if conflicting pattern already registered
- `deregister(pattern: &str) -> Option<SubjectSchema>`: remove a schema by pattern
- `lookup(subject: &str) -> Option<&SubjectSchema>`: find the most specific matching schema for a concrete subject
- `list_by_family(family: SubjectFamily) -> Vec<&SubjectSchema>`: list all schemas of a given family
- Thread-safe: concurrent lookups with serialized mutations (similar pattern to routing trie)

### Family Constraints Table
| Family          | Admissible Transforms    | Default Service Class | Reply Rule    | Evidence | Mobility  |
|-----------------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------------|----------|-----------|
| Event           | Map, Filter              | BestEffort           | None          | None     | Federated |
| Request         | Map                      | AtLeastOnce          | Required      | Log      | Federated |
| Reply           | None                     | AtLeastOnce          | N/A (is reply)| None     | Local     |
| Stream          | Map, Filter, Aggregate   | AtLeastOnce          | None          | Log      | Global    |
| Control         | None                     | BestEffort           | Optional      | Prove    | Local     |
| Service         | None                     | AtLeastOnce          | Optional      | Log      | Local     |
| CaptureSelector | Filter                   | BestEffort           | None          | None     | Local     |

## File Location
src/messaging/subject.rs

## Tests
- Compile valid schemas for each family: verify all fields populated correctly
- Compile invalid schemas: Reply without ReplySpaceRule -> error, Control not in $SYS -> error, CaptureSelector without wildcard -> error, Event with ExactlyOnce -> error, Request without reply_space_rule -> error
- SubjectRegistry: register, lookup, deregister, list_by_family
- Lookup specificity: more specific pattern wins over less specific
- Concurrent registry operations: concurrent register/lookup from multiple threads
- Transform validation: verify family constrains admissible transforms (e.g., Event cannot have Aggregate transform)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:21:30.218350212Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-17T07:21:30.218350212Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fabric","semantic","subject"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2.3","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T07:21:30.218350212Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.2.4","title":"[FABRIC][P1.4] Unit tests for subject fabric","description":"## Overview
Comprehensive unit and integration tests for the subject fabric (P1.1 routing, P1.2 sharding, P1.3 semantic families). This task depends on P1.1, P1.2, and P1.3 being complete.

## Requirements

### Routing Correctness (30+ test cases)
Test the trie-based routing engine from P1.1:
- Literal match: exact match returns subscriber, non-match returns empty
- Single wildcard (`*`): `foo.*` matches `foo.bar`, not `foo.bar.baz`
- Tail wildcard (`>`): `foo.>` matches `foo.bar` and `foo.bar.baz`
- Combined wildcards: `*.bar.>`, `foo.*.baz`, `foo.*.>`
- Multiple subscribers on same subject
- Overlapping patterns: `foo.bar`, `foo.*`, `foo.>` all match `foo.bar`
- Edge cases: empty subject handling, single-token subjects, very long subjects (100+ tokens)
- `>` alone matches all subjects
- `*` alone matches single-token subjects only
- No partial matches: `foo.bar` does not match `foo.bar.baz`
- Unsubscribe: remove subscriber, verify no longer in results
- Re-subscribe: unsubscribe then subscribe again, verify works correctly

### Queue Group Tests
- Single queue group: 3 subscribers in group `workers`, verify only 1 receives per lookup
- Multiple queue groups: groups `A` and `B` on same subject, verify 1 from each group
- Queue group removal: remove one member, verify remaining members still work
- Queue group + non-group: non-group subscribers AND group subscribers on same subject — non-group gets every message, group gets one-per-group
- Round-robin or distribution verification: over 100 lookups, all group members should receive roughly equal share

### Cancel-Correctness Tests
- Subscribe via guard, drop guard, verify subscriber removed from trie
- Subscribe via guard, cancel the Cx, verify subscriber removed
- Subscribe multiple, cancel one, verify only the cancelled one is removed
- Rapid subscribe/cancel cycles: no ghost interest accumulates
- Concurrent cancel + lookup: cancel in one thread while looking up in another — lookup must never return the cancelled subscriber after the cancel completes

### Sharding Tests (P1.2)
- Distribution uniformity: 1000 random subjects, verify no shard >2x average
- Concurrent reads during writes: spawn readers and writers, verify no panics or incorrect results
- Generation counters: verify generation bumps on mutation, cache invalidation works
- Cross-shard independence: mutation on shard A does not affect shard B's generation or cache
- Same-shard concurrent access: multiple readers + one writer on same shard

### Semantic Family Tests (P1.3)
- Valid schema compilation for each of the 7 families
- Invalid schema rejection: Reply without ReplySpaceRule, Control not in $SYS, CaptureSelector without wildcard, Event+ExactlyOnce, Request without reply_space_rule
- Transform validation: each family only allows its admissible transforms
- SubjectRegistry CRUD: register, lookup, deregister, list_by_family
- Registry conflict detection: registering conflicting pattern returns error
- Lookup specificity: more specific pattern wins

### Performance Benchmarks
- Benchmark literal lookup (cached): target sub-microsecond
- Benchmark wildcard lookup: measure overhead vs literal
- Benchmark subscribe/unsubscribe throughput
- Benchmark concurrent lookup throughput (multi-threaded)

### Lab Runtime Integration
- Subject operations under deterministic scheduling (lab runtime)
- Verify cancel-correctness works with lab runtime's deterministic task cancellation
- Verify sharded lookups work correctly under lab runtime's controlled concurrency

### Test Quality
- All test failures include detailed logging: what was expected vs what was received
- Use `#[track_caller]` on assertion helpers for accurate failure locations
- Group tests into submodules: routing, queue_groups, cancel, sharding, semantic, perf, lab

## File Location
Tests in src/messaging/subject.rs (inline #[cfg(test)]) and/or tests/subject_fabric.rs

 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.20","title":"[FABRIC][CROSS] Strategic north star — five success criteria","description":"Document and enforce the five strategic success criteria that determine whether the Semantic Subject Fabric is worth building. Every design decision must be tested against these criteria.

## Five Success Criteria
1. **The public mental model MUST remain NATS-small** even as internal semantics get much richer. If a developer needs to understand subject cells, stewardship, or fountain coding to publish a message, the design has failed.
2. **Stronger guarantees MUST be named service classes, NOT hidden taxes on the common case.** The default (EphemeralInteractive) path must have zero overhead from durability, session types, or evidence recording. Stronger classes are opt-in and explicitly named.
3. **Every radical behavior MUST lower into small, inspectable artifacts**: tokens, certificates, leases, cut records, session contracts, key contexts, or explain plans. No magic — everything is tangible and debuggable.
4. **Autonomous policy loops MUST always run inside declared safety envelopes** with replay, evidence, and rollback. No autonomous action without declared bounds, recorded evidence, and the ability to undo.
5. **The first product wedge should be systems that need sovereignty, durable partial progress, and post-incident explanation at the same time.** This is the unique value proposition — not competing with NATS on simple pub/sub.

## Anti-Goal
A grand unified fabric that makes the common case slower, the rare case magical, and the operator story less legible than NATS would be a MISTAKE. This is the failure mode to actively guard against.

## Target
A fabric whose default path is simpler than today's stack, while its stronger paths are dramatically more auditable and compositional.

## Enforcement
- Document in src/messaging/mod.rs module-level docs
- Reference in every phase epic's description
- Every PR in the messaging module must state which criteria it advances
- Periodic review: does the current implementation still satisfy all 5?

## Tests
- No automated tests (this is a design-governance task)
- But every feature bead should reference which north star criteria it serves","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T16:43:11.958991782Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T06:19:58.841573622Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T06:19:58.841553315Z","close_reason":"Completed: documented the five FABRIC north-star criteria and anti-goal in src/messaging/mod.rs, and added the north-star reference block to every FABRIC phase epic description (asupersync-8w83i.1 through .17).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["criteria","fabric","strategy"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.20","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T16:43:11.958991782Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-8w83i.21","title":"[FABRIC][CROSS] Progressive disclosure model — layered API with no-footgun guarantees","description":"Design and enforce the progressive disclosure model for the fabric API. Users must be able to start simple and adopt stronger semantics incrementally, with each layer independently correct.

## Layer Definitions
- **Layer 0**: Connect, publish, subscribe — NATS-simple, EphemeralInteractive class, no obligations. This is the default. A user who never reads beyond Layer 0 docs should be productive and safe.
- **Layer 1**: Request/reply with timeouts — still simple, optional service obligations. Adds structured request/reply but no durability requirements.
- **Layer 2**: Durable streams, consumers, acks — DurableOrdered class, obligation-backed. Introduces persistence, consumer groups, and acknowledgment protocols.
- **Layer 3**: Session-typed protocols, service contracts, decision contracts — ObligationBacked/MobilitySafe classes. Introduces static protocol checking and formal service contracts.
- **Layer 4**: Counterfactual branches, intent compilation, forensic replay — ForensicReplayable class. Full reasoning-plane capabilities for post-incident analysis and what-if exploration.

## Critical Invariant
Layer 0 and Layer 1 MUST NOT become footguns that silently require Layer 3 or Layer 4 to be safe. Each layer must be independently correct within its declared service class. Specifically:
- Layer 0 publish MUST NOT silently drop messages in a way that only Layer 2 durability can prevent
- Layer 1 request/reply MUST NOT have failure modes that only Layer 3 session types can catch
- No accidental implementation leakage from higher layers into lower layers
- No performance tax from higher layers on lower layers

## Design Rules
- Default is always Layer 0 (EphemeralInteractive) unless explicitly upgraded
- Moving between layers is visible, attributable, and caused by declared DeliveryClass or ServiceContract
- Each layer's types are in separate modules (not all mixed together)
- Documentation for each layer stands alone — Layer 0 docs never reference Layer 4 concepts
- Error messages at each layer use that layer's vocabulary only

## Tests
- Layer 0 works without any higher-layer configuration or imports
- Each layer independently compiles and functions
- No type from Layer N+1 appears in Layer N's public API
- Performance benchmarks: Layer 0 has zero overhead from higher layers
- Feature flags can gate higher layers without breaking lower layers","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ChartreuseBeaver","created_at":"2026-03-17T16:43:49.504325358Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T06:38:58.374279763Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api","fabric","progressive-disclosure"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-8w83i.21","depends_on_id":"asupersync-8w83i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T16:43:49.504325358Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
